data.title: Zhipu AI breaks US chip reliance with first major model trained on Huawei stack
data.description:
Chinese artificial intelligence firm Zhipu AI said its new image generation model was trained on chips from Huawei Technologies, making it the first powerful open-source model to be developed on an entirely domestic training stack.
The Beijing-based company, fresh off its Hong Kong initial public offering, said on Wednesday that the achievement proved the feasibility of developing powerful multimodal models without US semiconductors, as Beijing pushes for self-reliance in China’s AI industry amid US export restrictions on cutting-edge chips.
According to Zhipu, the entire training pipeline for GLM-Image, from data preparation to the final training run, was conducted on Huawei’s Ascend Atlas 800T A2 server, incorporating the company’s in-house Ascend AI processors, and with MindSpore, Huawei’s all-in-one machine learning framework.
“We hope this can provide valuable reference for the community to explore the potential of domestic computing power,” Zhipu said.
Powerful multimodal AI models that can natively process text, voice, image and video are widely seen by industry experts as the next frontier of AI models.

Zhipu’s model has a hybrid architecture made up of both autoregressive and diffusion elements, a design that enables the multimodal capabilities pioneered by Google DeepMind’s Nano Banana Pro, which can accurately generate both images and text.
Zhipu claimed the model achieved industry-leading scores among open-source models for text rendering and was especially powerful at Chinese character generation. However, the self-reported scores showed that the model still lagged behind ByteDance’s proprietary image generation model Seedream 4.5.
ByteDance has not disclosed what chips were used to train its model.
On Tuesday, the US government gave the green light for Nvidia to sell its second most powerful chip, the H200, to China after months of lobbying by the company.
However, Beijing has told customs agents to prevent the import of the chip and told tech companies that it would only approve H200 orders in exceptional circumstances, Reuters reported on Wednesday.
Huawei’s Ascend chips have proven effective at training smaller models like Zhipu’s GLM-Image, but their efficacy for training the company’s flagship series of large language models, such as the next-generation GLM-5, was still to be determined, according to a person familiar with the matter.
Nvidia chips still dominate cutting-edge model development in China, with the few Chinese AI models publicly disclosed to have been trained on domestic chips still limited to second-tier model players such as iFlytek, which was blacklisted by the US government in 2019. Zhipu was added to the same export control list in January last year.
Beijing has also encouraged Chinese tech firms to switch to domestic machine learning frameworks such as Huawei’s MindSpore instead of TensorFlow and PyTorch, developed by Google and Meta Platforms respectively.
However, MindSpore’s adoption remained limited compared with the more established US frameworks, according to data from various open-source developer platforms.

data.title: Why China may buy Nvidia’s H200 chips as Trump eases curbs – and replace them later
data.description:
China is likely to accept Nvidia’s H200 artificial intelligence (AI) chips following US clearance, driven by immediate demand in the country’s vast tech sector, according to analysts – though they said Beijing would push to replace them over time.
The American chip giant’s second-most-advanced AI processor can be exported to China from Thursday, as long as shipments total no more than half the amount sold in the US, according to the Department of Commerce’s Bureau of Industry and Security.
Washington restricted H200 exports to China about a year ago, at the end of the Biden administration, to prevent other countries from accessing advanced American technology.
Those curbs were eased under US President Donald Trump, whose government has sought to strike a balance between concerns over China’s AI progress and maintaining the global market share of US firms.
Chinese developers of AI-powered gadgets are likely to welcome the reversal, especially those already using compatible Nvidia chips, as the country currently lacks equally sophisticated alternatives, analysts said. The chips could also support production in sectors such as advanced vehicles and high-performance computers.
“China is behind, so the market is going to want these chips,” said Charles Chang, a finance professor at Fudan University in Shanghai. “I don’t think there’s going to be a whole lot of questions. It’s an easier move for people to say ‘hey, let’s go buy these chips and they already work with the ones we have’.”
However, analysts agreed that Beijing’s long-term push for self-sufficiency would remain even if the H200 proves popular. Officials are expected to monitor inflows and may eventually step in to encourage greater use of domestic alternatives as local technology matures.


The government could, for example, impose quotas on US chip imports or specify which companies are eligible to buy them, Chang said.
Jayant Menon, visiting senior fellow at ISEAS – Yusof Ishak Institute in Singapore, said Beijing has a “clear plan for its chip industry, which includes strong support for building independent capabilities”.
“Any easing of sales of chips from the US will not noticeably affect long-term domestic plans or capacity.”
“In short, the ball remains in China’s court, and [it] is unlikely to be derailed by decisions made by the US.”
While China remains heavily dependent on foreign technology in some cutting-edge areas, it is making headway towards semiconductor self-sufficiency – a goal, backed by state investment, that gained urgency in 2022 after Washington imposed export restrictions on high-end chips.
The Chinese semiconductor industry should see a 7.8 per cent compound annual growth rate from 2025 to 2034, potentially reaching US$381.24 billion, the China-Britain Business Council forecast in June.
As of the fourth quarter last year, more than half of the AI chips used in Chinese data centres alone were domestically made, the market research firm TrendForce found.




“China is becoming more and more independent in coming up with its own chips,” said Victor Gao, vice-president of the Centre for China and Globalisation think tank in Beijing. The more the US restricts China, the more “innovative” it becomes, he added.
“So, you are faced with a very complicated situation. If China is to become [technologically] independent, why should they use these [H200] chips?”
Alfredo Montufar-Helu, China-based managing director at Ankura Consulting, agreed that as locally made chips improve in quality, China is “unlikely to encourage a dependency that undermines its long-term security goals”.
Beijing may also be closely watching the bipartisan Remote Access Security Act, which targets China and was passed by the US House of Representatives on Monday. The bill expands federal oversight to restrict foreign access to sensitive technology, including AI chips, by extending export controls to remote access and cloud-based exposure.
Defending the legislation, US House Representative John Moolenaar said in a statement that “the Chinese Communist Party’s AI ambitions are being fuelled” by access to American chips housed in data centres outside China.
Allowing H200 sales in China while still blocking remote access to Nvidia’s more advanced Blackwell-series AI chips would follow the spirit of the bill, Montufar-Helu said – though the act still needs US Senate approval and a presidential nod.
“The legislative intent is clear – draw a hard line at the absolute cutting edge to ensure Chinese AI development remains a generation behind,” he said.

data.title: Cambodia says Chen Zhi’s extradition to China ‘not the end’ of scam centre crackdown
data.description:
Cambodia’s arrest of alleged scam centre kingpin Chen Zhi and his extradition to China is “not the end” of ‍the Southeast Asian nation’s battle to stamp out transborder crimes, its foreign minister said.
Last week’s surprise arrest of Chinese-born Chen is a key step in an until now fragmented international campaign targeting sophisticated scam networks in Southeast Asia run by criminal gangs, swindling victims worldwide out of billions of dollars.
The extradition of Chen, sanctioned by ⁠several countries and indicted by the United States for wire fraud and money laundering, followed a joint investigation by China and Cambodia, details of which have not been made public.
“It’s a continued combat, and we have set measures and steps in order to eradicate this crime,” Cambodian Foreign Minister Prak Sokhonn said in a rare interview from Phnom Penh, the capital.
Cambodia has always been determined to crack down on transnational crimes, especially crimes employing new technology, ‍such as online scam organisations, he said.
“The fact that Chen was arrested and extradited to China is just reflecting this firm ‍commitment of Cambodia to combat the crime. And it’s not the end of the combat.”
He gave no details of the investigation into Chen but said full cooperation with Beijing had started months ago.


Billionaire conglomerate head
Chen, an enigmatic ‌billionaire in his late 30s, heads the Prince Group conglomerate, based out of Cambodia with scores of ostensibly legitimate businesses worldwide.
But US authorities have said ‍those were fronts for “one of the largest investment fraud operations in history”.
Last year, Prince Group rejected as baseless the accusations against Chen.
Hong Kong, Singapore and Taiwan have frozen hundreds of millions of dollars in assets linked to Prince Group, while Britain and South Korea have imposed sanctions.
Last year, US prosecutors seized about US$15 billion in bitcoin linked to Chen and Cambodia has liquidated ‌the Prince Bank ⁠he founded.
It is not clear what Chen will be charged with in China, which set up a special task force in 2020 to investigate Prince Group, Chinese court documents dating to 2022 showed. Last week, China’s state broadcaster showed video of Chen arriving in Beijing handcuffed and hooded.

It said Chen was wanted for fraud and running illegal casinos and described him as the “leader of a major transnational gambling and fraud crime syndicate”.
The ‌scams, operated from vast compounds in countries such as Cambodia, Myanmar and Laos, involve tens of thousands of workers.
Among them are human trafficking victims lured by promises of jobs in industries such as technology and hospitality, forced to cheat strangers online or face brutal punishment.
Cambodia had “very close cooperation” on transnational ‌crime with countries such as the United States, China, South Korea and neighbours Vietnam and Thailand, the Cambodian minister said.
“This is the result of a long investigation,” Prak Sokhonn added, referring to the events that led to Chen’s extradition.
“Chen Zhi had Cambodian nationality, but when we made those investigations and found that his nationality was not legally obtained and he is also a Chinese national … we have decided to extradite him to China.”

data.title: Hong Kong officials urge buy-back plan over ‘challenging’ Wang Fuk Court rebuild
data.description:
Rebuilding the fire-ravaged Wang Fuk Court on its original site to rehouse displaced residents will be “immensely challenging” because of complex legal and property rights issues, a senior Hong Kong official has said, adding that a cash buy-back would be the “fastest and most flexible” solution.
Deputy Financial Secretary Michael Wong Wai-lun, who led the government task force on emergency resettlement, has shifted his stance several times over the past few days.
Initially describing in-situ redevelopment as “not practical”, over the weekend he later acknowledged residents’ wishes on Monday. By Wednesday, however, he again stressed the significant challenges involved.
“Assuming building new towers requires four to five years and demolition takes 1½ years, this means we need to handle property rights and legal issues in a large number of flats within some three years’ time, which is considerably challenging,” he said, adding that on-site redevelopment could take a decade.
At the Legislative Council’s first meeting since the fire that claimed 161 lives seven weeks ago, Wong pledged that the government “must allow residents to have choices”, emphasising that their responses to a government survey would not solely determine their future housing arrangements.
Authorities last week launched a survey of all displaced residents, setting out nine rehousing options, including a “flat-for-flat” exchange or a cash buyout.
Wong described the cash buy-back as the “fastest and most flexible” option for thousands affected. He also pointed to a site earmarked for Home Ownership Scheme (HOS) flats on Chung Nga Road West in Tai Po, which could provide up to 1,500 units by 2029.
Housing Secretary Winnie Ho Wing-yin dismissed concerns that allocating subsidised housing to fire victims would lengthen the public housing queue, noting that the supply of subsidised flats over the next five years was expected to reach around 30,000 units per year.

Ho said a structural assessment of the seven fire-damaged blocks found that demolition was highly likely, even though there was no immediate risk of collapse.
She said the blaze’s extreme heat had severely compromised the reinforced concrete, leaving it exposed, warped and bent.
“There is significant uncertainty as to whether these reinforced concrete components can continue to meet the standards for long-term structural durability, load-bearing capacity, fire safety and waterproofing,” Ho said.
At the centre of the debate is the resettlement plan, with dozens of lawmakers opposing on-site redevelopment and urging a swift rehousing scheme.
Carmen Kan Wai-mun said such a move would create legal uncertainty and entail significant time costs, citing the reconstruction of London’s Grenfell Tower following the deadly 2017 fire, which took nine years to turn into a memorial site. She argued that in-situ redevelopment is “very difficult in practice”.
“Without a unified ownership, how can the Wang Fuk Court be demolished and rebuilt? The disposition rights over common areas and the rights of use of insurance also involve complex legal issues,” Kan said.
Fellow lawmaker Andrew Lam Siu-lo, a veteran town planner, also cautioned against the proposal, stressing the need for swift rehousing and described the chances of quickly recovering all property rights as “close to zero”.
“If the government fails to do so, it will eventually have to reclaim them compulsorily by law, thus triggering the statutory compensation mechanism,” Lam said, adding that public concern for displaced residents should not be conflated with support for compensation funded by public money.
Augustine Wong Ho-ming, representing the real estate and construction sector, echoed this view, saying it would be impossible for private developers to undertake the redevelopment given the high land premium costs and the likelihood that the tragedy would significantly depress selling prices.

data.title: Huawei reclaims top spot over Apple in China smartphone market after chip breakthrough
data.description:
Huawei Technologies edged out Apple to reclaim the No 1 spot in mainland China’s smartphone market in 2025, a new report showed, as the domestic handset maker overcame crippling US sanctions.
For the full year, Huawei captured 16.4 per cent of China’s smartphone market with 46.7 million units shipped, narrowly beating Apple, which shipped 46.2 million iPhones for a 16.2 per cent share, research firm International Data Corporation (IDC) said on Wednesday.
Huawei’s smartphone shipments fell 1.9 per cent compared with the previous year, while Apple enjoyed a 4 per cent growth, IDC said. It was the first time Huawei had secured a full-year lead on the mainland’s smartphone market since 2020, after the US tightened sanctions on the company and cut off its access to advanced chips.
The Shenzhen-based company turned to self-designed, China-made processors to power its smartphones, including the latest Kirin 9030 in its premium flagship model Mate 80 Pro Max.
“The continuous improvement in Huawei’s in-house chip production was a key driver in 2025, providing the necessary support for its shipment momentum,” said Will Wong, senior research manager for client devices at IDC in Asia-Pacific.
Wong said the competitive landscape had changed in China, as some domestic players stepped up their overseas push by shifting focus to markets such as India and Africa to avoid direct rivalry with a resurgent Huawei at home.

Chinese firm Vivo was just behind Apple after shipping 46.1 million units, followed by domestic rivals Xiaomi and Oppo.
China, the world’s largest smartphone market, posted a 0.6 per cent decline compared with the previous year, shipping a total of 284.6 million units in 2025, IDC said. It expected the decline to widen in 2026 due to the memory chip crunch.
“Rising memory costs are placing significant pressure on smartphone [manufacturers], and this will remain a critical challenge throughout 2026,” Wong said.
Chinese smartphone makers faced mounting pressure from the global memory supply chain, as leading manufacturers prioritised capacity for clients amid surging demand from artificial intelligence applications and infrastructure.
Xiaomi and Honor announced plans to raise prices of smartphones and tablets, while Meizu was forced to cancel the launch of a new handset under cost pressure.
A strong premium presence would give brands like Huawei and Apple a crucial advantage in 2026, as high-end margins helped absorb rising production costs, Wong added.
The impact of the memory price hikes was already reflected in the fourth quarter, as domestic players cut shipments of budget-priced models to protect their bottom lines, driving the volume down by 0.8 per cent.
Apple, however, surged 21.5 per cent in the December quarter in China, banking on the release of its popular iPhone 17 series, and captured the top spot with a 21 per cent share of the market in the period, according to IDC.
Globally, Apple retained its position as the largest smartphone vendor in 2025 with a 19.7 per cent share, ahead of Samsung Electronics’ 19.1 per cent, IDC data showed. Xiaomi, Vivo and Oppo ranked third, fourth and fifth worldwide.

data.title: Malaysian gets 12 years’ jail in Singapore for Caucasian ‘sugar daddy’ sextortion scam
data.description:
A Singapore court has sentenced a 38-year-old Malaysian man to 12 years in prison and 15 strokes of the cane for posing online as a wealthy Caucasian “sugar daddy” and coercing women into sex while extorting money with threats to release explicit material.
The man, Rajwant Singh Gill Narajan Singh, was convicted of two counts of cheating and one count each of extortion and attempted extortion involving two victims, identified in court documents as PW1 and PW2.
“By his horrific and perverse conduct, purely intended to satisfy both his sexual and material desires, the accused has callously and irreparably damaged the lives of his victims,” Deputy Public Prosecutor Jeremy Bin told the court, as quoted by The Straits Times.
Court accounts reported that Gill adopted multiple online personas, including “Mike” and other names, portraying himself as a white man with a lavish lifestyle and offering large monthly sums in return for sex and companionship.
The women were pressured into sending lewd photos or videos, then invited to Malaysia, where Gill allegedly appeared in person as the “sugar daddy’s” driver, using that role to gauge whether to cultivate sympathy or resort to threats relayed through the fictional benefactor.
One victim handed over more than S$183,000 (US$142,000), according to court reporting, after being threatened that explicit videos would be shared unless she transferred money, continued engaging with him sexually and complied with further instructions designed to maintain his control over her.
Gill was arrested in a joint covert operation involving the Singapore Police Force and the Royal Malaysian Police Force, and was convicted in November.
While this case has concluded, Gill still faces pending charges involving 13 other alleged victims.
In a separate case involving another woman, he was sentenced last year to 4½ years’ jail and ordered to pay S$7,000 in compensation after pleading guilty to cheating offences, The Straits Times reported.

The prosecution’s case unfolded against the backdrop of the so-called sugar dating trend, in which typically younger partners are offered money, gifts or lifestyle support by older or wealthier people in return for companionship that may or may not include sex.
In both Singapore and Malaysia, the practice occupies a legal grey area, with authorities and lawyers warning that arrangements can quickly cross into criminal territory when payment is explicitly tied to sexual acts, when deception is used to obtain consent or when threats and exploitation are involved.
The issue drew regional attention in 2021 with the controversy surrounding Sugarbook, a Malaysia-based “sugar dating” platform that claimed to connect mutually consenting adults but was accused by critics and authorities of facilitating transactional sex. Malaysian police questioned the platform’s founder, while the country’s communications regulator moved to block access to the service, citing concerns over public morality and potential links to prostitution.
Singapore courts have since dealt with a string of cases in which offenders used “sugar daddy” arrangements as a facade for fraud, sexual exploitation and extortion.
In one 2021 case, a man was jailed for orchestrating what a judge described as an “elaborate and well-executed plan” to cheat women by posing as a wealthy benefactor. Several other men received jail sentences last year in similar cases involving the targeting of teenagers and threats to circulate explicit images to exert control over their victims.

data.title: Wicked stars’ incredible costumes among exhibits at Oscar-winning designer’s new show
data.description:
Long before audiences saw Glinda’s dazzling pink dresses or Elphaba’s dramatic layered ensembles on the big screen, Paul Tazewell was imagining how silhouette, colour and texture would bring the witches of Oz to life.
Tazewell, the award-winning costume designer behind both Wicked films, the stage musical Hamilton and Steven Spielberg’s West Side Story, will be the focus of a new exhibition opening on January 19 at the Griffin Museum of Science and Industry in Chicago, Illinois.
“Crafting Character: The Costumes of Paul Tazewell” in the museum’s Kenneth C Griffin Studio will showcase designs all the way from early projects in Tazewell’s career to some of his most recognisable pieces in film, theatre, television and the red carpet.
“To allow visitors to engage directly with the work, to see it up close and in person, I’m hopeful that it will be exciting for them,” Tazewell says. “I think overall it would be a very full experience for anybody that chooses to come and see it.”

It is the designer’s debut exhibition and Tazewell says being approached by the museum was a blessing. His retrospective is near the museum’s 56th annual “Black Creativity Juried Art Exhibition”, which highlights black innovators and artists.
“I am so honoured that my exhibition sits adjacent to that as a celebration of the artist community here in Chicago,” he says.
Last year, Tazewell made history as the first black man to win the Academy Award for costume design, for his work on the first Wicked film – one of many milestones featured in the exhibition.
Visitors will follow a multimedia journey through Tazewell’s three-decade career and meticulous creative process. Each room will be accompanied by his audio narration, along with behind-the-scenes videos, original sketches and photographs.

The exhibition opens with a room centred on Tazewell’s inspirations, bringing together family photos, artwork by his mother and costume sketches from his college years.
Janelle Monáe’s viral 2025 Met Gala look, created by Tazewell in collaboration with designer Thom Browne, anchors the space, but a photo of Tazewell in high school marks the true beginning of his Ozian trajectory.
As a child growing up in Akron, Ohio, he was captivated by the shift from sepia-toned Kansas to vibrant Technicolor in the 1939 film The Wizard of Oz, a scene that still inspires him. At 16, he designed costumes for his school production of The Wiz. He would later win an Emmy in 2016 for costume design for The Wiz: Live! before moving on to the screen adaptation of the stage musical Wicked.
As the retrospective continues, viewers will see the intricate engineering behind Tazewell’s designs.
Anita’s yellow dress from West Side Story appears to ripple effortlessly as Ariana DeBose spins during the “America” sequence. Tazewell says numerous technical decisions went into achieving the effect.
“There’s geometry that goes into the pattern making. There are choices about fabrics that are specific to textile fibres and what those different aspects bring to manifesting costumes.”

Other signature pieces include costumes from Broadway’s Hamilton – from Thomas Jefferson’s purple suit to King George III’s royal ensemble and the Schuyler sisters’ Winter Ball gowns. Tazewell won his first Tony Award for the musical’s costume design, though he emphasises the contributions of the artisans who were on his team, such as tailor Artur Allakhverdyan.
In a room dedicated to collaboration, the exhibition spotlights the craftspeople behind Tazewell’s designs, including Miodrag Guberinic, who assisted him on the Wicked films, and Mark Zappone, who collaborated on The Sleeping Beauty ballet production for Pacific Northwest Ballet last year.
“There are many minds behind creating and realising a costume,” Tazewell says. “I’ve had the privilege to design alongside incredibly skilled makers.”
The final room of the exhibition is devoted to Tazewell’s work on Wicked. His most ambitious project to date, he designed more than 1,000 pieces for both films. In the first film, there were 25 costumes for Cynthia Erivo’s Elphaba and another 25 for Ariana Grande’s Glinda.

Tazewell wanted to capture the personalities of Elphaba and Glinda in what they wore, as he aims to do with all of his projects.
“There is an intention behind both conceptually what the costume is and how the character will be represented in the costume,” he says.
Glinda’s effervescent pink bubble dress is not only a nod to the original Wizard of Oz dress, but it is also meant to reflect an archetype of goodness. Made from layered nylon and silk organza, the dress comprises 137 pattern pieces and around 20,000 beads.
On the other hand, many of Elphaba’s dark ensembles, such as the one she wears while singing “Defying Gravity”, have mushroom-inspired micro pleats and layered textures that reflect her affinity to nature and animals.
For Tazewell, the exhibition is about more than seeing his designs up close.
“I hope it inspires young people to want to be a costume designer, and at the very least, that it inspires creativity,” he says. “To be that kind of inspiration is really, I think, a big part of why I’m here, why I’m here on Earth.”

data.title: 73% of Hong Kong, Greater Bay Area firms speeding up expansion into Asean: survey
data.description:
Nearly three-quarters of enterprises from Hong Kong and Greater Bay Area cities will speed up their expansion into the Asean market as a primary strategy to mitigate risks from international trade frictions and shifting global supply chains, according to a survey.
The survey and research findings, co-published by the Hong Kong Trade Development Council and Singapore-based United Overseas Bank on Wednesday, also revealed that about 98 per cent of enterprises are planning to keep or expand their operations in the Southeast Asian region.
The survey, carried out from July to August 2025, polled 600 companies based in Hong Kong and five key cities in the bay area: Guangzhou, Shenzhen, Foshan, Dongguan and Zhongshan.
“With the shifting global supply chain landscape and tariff uncertainties, many Greater Bay Area enterprises are accelerating their Asean engagement not merely as a means of risk diversification, but to capture new growth opportunities in fast-evolving markets,” Chu Wing-chor, the council’s deputy director of research, said.
“Hong Kong’s unique positioning as a superconnector allows businesses to leverage the city’s comprehensive trade, financial and professional services platform to navigate this transformation efficiently.”
According to the survey findings, 73 per cent of bay area respondents planned to accelerate their business expansion efforts in Asean countries, underlining the region’s significance as a pivotal and increasingly pressing growth driver amid global trade tensions.
The research highlighted a significant 25 percentage point rise from the previous year in the number of bay area firms looking to either expand or maintain their sales activities within the Asean, with 98 per cent of the companies surveyed still focusing on these vibrant sales markets.
Asean economies were progressively gaining importance, the study found, with Singapore, Vietnam, Thailand, Malaysia and Indonesia emerging as primary sites for bay area enterprises in the next three years.
To facilitate their expansion strategies, businesses anticipated dedicating an average of 30 per cent more resources towards their Asean growth initiatives.
Vietnam stood out as the top priority for increased investment, with companies aiming for a 47 per cent boost in resources, followed by Indonesia at 37 per cent, and Thailand and Malaysia each at 32 per cent.

The overarching strategic focus revolved around bolstering sales growth, particularly in Thailand, Vietnam and Indonesia, while broadening firms’ production and sourcing hubs, with Vietnam, Thailand and Malaysia being the preferred locations.
Despite Singapore’s already strong presence among enterprises in bay area cities, respondents said they would still aim to increase investments by an average of 23 per cent to speed up business expansion, notably in financing and regional office set-ups, in the city state.
Gary Ng Cheuk-yan, a senior economist at Natixis Corporate and Investment Bank, said that while no country could fully hide from protectionism and geopolitical tensions, Asean was the most feasible shelter from the US-China trade war.
“For Greater Bay Area firms, Asean offers the advantages of rising potential growth, close geographic proximity and access to the Chinese diaspora, which may make it easier to form partnerships,” he said.
“However, they should be wary of risks posed by government red tape, regulatory fragmentation and complexity, local competition, cultural differences, currency volatility and underdeveloped infrastructure.”
Danny Lau Tat-pong, honorary chairman of the Hong Kong Small and Medium Enterprises Association and managing director of Kam Pin Industrial, a Hong Kong-based architectural metal fabricator known for its aluminium products, held a more cautious view on the feasibility of replacing Western demand with Southeast Asian consumers.
“Asean countries have their own production. There are some products they don’t produce themselves, or they do but the quality is not that good; in that case, your chances might be stronger. But if your price is not low and your quality is about the same, your competitiveness will be very weak,” he said.
“To develop a new market, you have to mobilise a lot of resources, including money and manpower before you can develop the local market with a lot of promotional efforts.”
Lau also highlighted a stark disparity in potential trade volume, warning that even if a business successfully broke into the Asean market, it may find sales volume to be much lower than in the US.

data.title: China’s anti-corruption watchdog reveals how former banker fell in bribery scandal
data.description:
China’s anti-corruption watchdog has detailed how disgraced former banker and Communist Party chief of Hubei province Jiang Chaoliang received bribes for favours, ultimately landing himself and his brothers under investigation.
The revelations came in an episode of a CCTV documentary series that aired on Tuesday night during the fifth plenary session of the Central Commission for Discipline Inspection (CCDI).
Held at the beginning of each year, the session aims to sum up the achievements of President Xi Jinping’s sweeping anti-corruption campaign and lay out areas to focus on in the new year.
On Wednesday, the closing communique of the plenary session called for maintaining “high-pressure deterrence” against corruption.
It said that “new and hidden forms of corruption” would be targeted.
It also pledged that anti-corruption authorities would “intensify efforts to investigate cross-border corruption cases” and cooperate with the top legislature in formulating a law against cross-border corruption.
Jiang, 68, was sent to the southern province of Guangdong during the 1997 Asian financial crisis. While there, he worked alongside then vice-governor Wang Qishan to manage the fallout. Wang would go on to become the future vice-president of China.
Jiang later occupied several high-ranking positions within the central bank and the financial industry, including chairman and party secretary of the Bank of Communications, vice-chairman and president of China Development Bank, and chairman and party secretary of the Agricultural Bank of China (ABC).


According to the CCTV documentary, businessman Li Yuanguang spent over a decade cultivating close ties with Jiang’s family, starting from when Jiang was a mid-level ABC executive.
Li paid for Jiang’s children’s education and medical care for his parents. He even gave money to Jiang’s mother for what Li called “mahjong funds” and financed a property purchase for the family’s nanny.
The businessman’s long game paid off when Jiang, who became ABC chairman in 2011, helped Li’s company secure a massive automated teller machine (ATM) procurement contract.
Central to the corruption scheme were Jiang’s two younger brothers: Jiang Binliang and Jiang Zhongliang.
They leveraged their brother’s influence, according to the report, with Jiang Zhongliang admitting to acting as a matchmaker for deals, while Jiang Binliang enjoyed a lavish lifestyle funded by the graft.
Neither held official positions but both became conduits for bribes, as businessmen funnelled millions of yuan through them for projects and approvals.
Li even invited Jiang’s brothers to “invest” in his company at no cost, registering them as shareholders without requiring them to provide funds. In return, Jiang Chaoliang helped Li secure contracts and other benefits.
Jiang Chaoliang was appointed governor of the northeastern province of Jilin in 2014 and was promoted to party chief of central Hubei province two years later.
He remained as Hubei party chief until February 2020, when he was removed as Beijing authorities responded to public anger over what was seen as the botched handling of the Covid-19 pandemic outbreak, which began in Wuhan, the provincial capital.
The Jiang family story ended with Jiang Chaoliang’s corruption investigation in February of last year and subsequent expulsion from the party and public office.
His suspected crimes were referred to prosecutors for investigation, and those who helped him or gave him bribes have faced consequences, according to the documentary. But it did not reveal whether Jiang’s brothers had been punished yet.
Jiang is among the most prominent figures caught in the party’s decade-long anti-corruption campaign, which snared a record 65 high-ranking officials last year.
Those detained included top provincial and city officials, ministry leaders, financial regulators, bankers, state enterprise executives, university presidents and military top brass.
The CCDI plenum communique said China’s anti-corruption investigators would continue to crack down on misconduct in sectors including finance, state-owned enterprises, energy, education, academic societies, development zones and public procurement.
The key sectors last year were finance, state-owned enterprises, energy, fire safety, tobacco, pharmaceuticals, universities, sports, public welfare and charity, construction projects and bidding processes.
A Wednesday commentary by party mouthpiece People’s Daily called for “putting power in the cage in a more scientific way”, citing a recent speech by Xi.
It added that the party would amend existing discipline rules to be more specific and feasible. It also said the party would step up efforts to make the rules equally binding for all “with no exception”.

data.title: Li Ka-shing’s CK Infrastructure gets nod to sell train leasing firm UK Rails
data.description:
CK Infrastructure Holdings is poised to complete the disposal of UK Rails (Eversholt Rail) this month following London’s approval of the deal, according to the company, which is part of Hong Kong tycoon Li Ka-shing’s business empire.
The train leasing company is owned by a consortium of the group’s companies: CK Infrastructure, which holds 65 per cent, along with CK Asset Holdings (20 per cent), Power Asset Holdings (10 per cent) and CK Hutchison Holdings (5 per cent), according to the group.
The deal, which had secured the nod of the UK’s Competition and Markets Authority, would “generate substantial revenue”, CK Infrastructure said on Wednesday.
The price was not disclosed, but earlier reports suggested that the group was seeking a valuation of as much as £4 billion (US$5.38 billion). The group acquired the asset in 2015 for £2.5 billion.
During CK Holdings’ interim results announcement in August, chairman Victor Li Tzar-kuoi said that the decision to sell the group’s interest in UK Rails would allow the “joint venture to unlock the potential value of the asset”.
“Only by buying and selling can a business grow bigger,” Li said. “We have never liked borrowing, so we reserve funds from time to time for larger transactions.”
Eversholt Rail is a top UK rail vehicle leasing company. It serves rail and freight operators by leasing them regional, short-distance and high-speed passenger trains, as well as freight locomotives, under long-term contracts.
The sale came after CK Infrastructure agreed in August to pay around £350 million for 32 wind farms in the UK.
In April, it formed a consortium with CK Asset and Power Assets to acquire Lionrai Investments No 1, which owns 100 per cent of Northern Ireland natural gas network Phoenix Energy from NatWest Group Pension Fund and Utilities Trust of Australia for £757 million.
Phoenix Energy is one of three gas distribution network operators in Northern Ireland. Its network covers nearly half of the local population, including Greater Belfast, and accounts for 78 per cent of gas connections in Northern Ireland.

Two weeks later in May, CK bought UU Solar, which owns and operates 70 renewable power-generation assets in the UK, for £90.8 million.
In March last year, CK Hutchison, one of the flagship companies of the group, announced its bid to sell an 80 per cent stake in its overseas port assets to a consortium led by US firm BlackRock and Terminal Investment, the terminal arm of the world’s largest container shipper, Mediterranean Shipping.
The deal, valued at about US$23 billion, would encompass more than 40 terminal ports across more than 20 countries.
The transaction, however, has yet to be completed following Beijing’s opposition to the sale amid trade disputes with Washington.
